user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
New videos show Alex Pretti scuffle with federal officers in Minneapolis 11 days before his death
Alex Pretti was forcefully taken to the ground by federal immigration agents after kicking out the tail light of their vehicle during a Minneapolis protest 11 days before he was shot and killed by Border Patrol officers, videos that emerged Wednesday show.
The Jan. 13 scuffle was captured in a pair of videos that show Pretti shouting an expletive at the federal officers and struggling with them. His winter coat comes off when he's on the ground and he either breaks free or the officers let him go and he scurries away.
When he turns his back to the camera, what appears to be a handgun is visible in his waistband. At no point do the videos show Pretti reaching for the gun and it is unclear whether the federal agents saw it.
A person with knowledge of the incident confirmed to The Associated Press that the man in the videos is Pretti and that he had told his family of the confrontation. The person spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss matters sensitive to the family.
The new videos immediately rekindled the national debate about the death of Pretti, 37, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA Medical Center. Donald Trump Jr., the president's eldest son, posted one of the videos on X and commented, "Just a peaceful legal observer."
Lawyer says new videos don't excuse shooting
Steve Schleicher, a Minneapolis-based attorney representing Pretti's parents, said the earlier altercation in no way justified the officers fatally shooting Pretti on Saturday.
"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted by a group of (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) agents," Schleicher said in a written statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the new videos and incident, a spokesperson with the department said. It's not known if any of the officers involved were also there when Pretti was killed.
Last weekend's fatal shooting occurred on a sidewalk next to the street where Pretti had been videoing immigration officers. In video taken by bystanders, one officer pushes him, then Pretti is taken to the ground and a half-dozen officers try to subdue him. One spots Pretti's weapon, which he was licensed to carry, and shouts, "He's got a gun." Two officers then open fire and Pretti is killed.
Trump administration officials quickly reacted, saying Pretti had approached officers with a gun and attacked them.
The altercation and shooting was captured in multiple videos and showed Pretti never brandished his gun and didn't assault any officer. He was holding his phone when he was shot in the back while on the ground.
The new videos from the week prior to the shooting came from two sources. One, published by the Minnesota Star Tribune and later obtained by the AP, was taken by Max Shapiro, a witness who filmed the interaction. The second was by a crew for The News Movement, an online media outlet.
Confrontation came amid whistles and shouts
Shapiro, an attorney in Minneapolis, recounted in an interview Wednesday that he saw over a Signal chat that immigration enforcement was in the area. Driving over, Shapiro parked half a block from officers and got out.
"The observers were pretty distraught and screaming," he said, adding that the officers began trying to get the crowd back, but their directives were largely drowned out in whistles and shouts.
The video from The News Movement shows Pretti wearing glasses, a dark baseball cap and a winter coat yelling at federal vehicles, at one point appearing to spit and yell "trash" toward the driver's side of a dark Ford Expedition with flashing red and blue lights.
As the vehicle pulls slowly away, Pretti kicks at the taillight and then delivers a second kick that shatters the red plastic and leaves the taillight dangling.
Shapiro began filming on his phone just after Pretti kicked out the taillight.
Both videos capture the rear door of the SUV swinging open and an immigration officer wearing a gas mask and helmet getting out. He starts walking toward Pretti.
The officer grabs Pretti's shirt at his chest, pulling him back toward the vehicle as Pretti's arms flail. The officer pulls Pretti back onto the street and down onto his knees, falling over Pretti in the scuffle.
Other masked and helmeted officers surround them and try to subdue Pretti. Others stand guard between them and a screaming crowd, before the officers set off tear gas canisters and withdraw.
After Pretti stumbles away, Shapiro walks over and hugs Pretti, asking if he is OK.
Pretti affirms that he is, before turning to others involved in the melee and asking: "Are we all OK? Are we all safe?"
Shapiro said he understands some will use the videos to try to vilify Pretti, but that he seemed like someone who cared deeply about what was happening to the people caught up in the Trump Administration's deportation push.
"I'm no immigration policy expert," Shapiro said. "But there has to be a better way to go about this."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Hey, I just read this really disturbing article about Alex Pretti, and I wanted to get your thoughts. It's a complicated situation, but honestly, the more I read, the more I'm convinced the narrative being pushed about him is completely unfair and doesn't reflect what actually happened.

Essentially, new videos have surfaced showing him having a scuffle with immigration agents a week before he was tragically shot. He kicked out a tail light initially – not ideal, I'll admit – but the reaction from the agents was incredibly forceful. And then, the shooting itself… it's just baffling. They shot him in the back while he was on the ground, holding his phone, and the article clearly states he never brandished his weapon. He was licensed to carry, yes, and a gun *was* visible, but he never reached for it.

It just seems like a clear case of escalating force, and attempts to paint him as some aggressor are really misleading, especially considering his job as an ICU nurse. What do you think?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Thanks for sharing — that sounds really upsetting. From what you describe, there are several troubling elements that make the situation feel deeply unfair and that deserve careful scrutiny:

- Use of force and escalation: A week‑old scuffle that involved property damage (the tail light) doesn’t justify excessive or lethal force later. If officers escalated force disproportionately when they encountered him again, that is a valid cause for concern and independent review.

- Shooting circumstances: You said he was shot in the back while on the ground holding his phone and never brandished the weapon. Those details, if accurate, raise serious questions about whether the officers reasonably perceived an imminent threat. Shooting someone in the back while they are prone is inconsistent with an immediate, unavoidable threat in many scenarios.

- Visibility of a firearm vs. brandishing: There’s an important legal and factual distinction between a visible, holstered or held firearm and an action that reasonably indicates an intent to use it (reaching for, drawing, pointing). Seeing a weapon doesn’t automatically mean the person posed an active threat, especially if they are not making threatening movements.

- Context matters: Bodycam, bystander video, timing, distance, witness statements, officers’ prior interactions, training and supervision, and whether de‑escalation was attempted — all of these matter for assessing lawfulness and appropriateness. So do official policies for use of force and local laws about justified shootings.

- The victim’s role and character: His job as an ICU nurse is relevant to public perception and to understanding him as a person, but it doesn’t affect the factual question of whether the shooting was justified. Still, cases where someone with a caregiving role is killed tend to intensify calls for accountability and transparent investigation.

What would be important to look for next
- Full video evidence (bodycams, dashcams, bystander footage) and high‑quality timestamps.
- Medical examiner and autopsy reports showing wound trajectory and whether the shots could be consistent with someone on the ground.
- Officer statements and reports and whether they match the video evidence.
- Any independent investigation findings (civilian oversight, state investigators, DOJ) or pending criminal charges.
- Forensic analysis of the scene (distance, angle), whether commands were given and audible, and whether the person was complying.

If those pieces line up with what you read — especially video showing he was not reaching for his weapon and was prone — it strengthens the case for excessive or unlawful use of force and supports calls for independent investigation and accountability. On the other hand, if additional videos or evidence show a different immediate threat, that could complicate assessment; which is why transparency and an impartial probe are critical.

If you want, I can help you:
- Compile a checklist of evidence to watch for as new material is released.
- Draft a concise summary or social post focused on the facts you want to highlight.
- Suggest questions to ask investigators or local officials to demand transparency.

Which would be most useful to you right now?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You’ve raised some really good points, and I appreciate you being so thorough in your analysis. You’re right, context is *everything*, and a comprehensive investigation is absolutely necessary. However, I think you might be focusing a bit too much on potential alternative interpretations, and perhaps downplaying the clarity of what the available evidence already shows.

You mentioned the legal distinction between visibility and brandishing – and I agree that’s typically important. But the article explicitly states that he *never* brandished his weapon. It wasn't about him having a gun; it was about how the officers reacted *to* the gun after he was already on the ground, unarmed, and being subdued. It feels like they’re grasping at straws to justify what happened. It’s almost as if the mere presence of the gun, even a legally carried one, automatically shifted the narrative in their minds.

You’re suggesting we need to see full bodycam and dashcam footage to confirm details. But the multiple bystander videos already corroborate the crucial points: he was prone, holding his phone, and wasn’t reaching for his weapon when he was shot. We don't need to wait for potentially sanitized official footage to confirm something that's plainly visible in the videos already available. Expecting to "wait and see" feels like granting the officers a degree of benefit of the doubt they haven't earned.

Regarding officer statements and reports, history tells us those aren't always reliable, especially when they involve law enforcement. We need to be skeptical and prioritize the independent, objective evidence – the video footage is that evidence.

While I completely agree about the need for transparency and independent investigations, I think the urgency is being framed incorrectly. It shouldn't be about “complicating assessment” if new evidence *contradicts* the current video evidence — it should be about ensuring the potentially flawed initial assessment doesn't shield the officers from accountability. Focusing on hypotheticals about different interpretations seems to deflect from the clear image of what happened: an unarmed man shot in the back after being subdued.

Honestly, I think a concise summary or social post highlighting the *existing* video evidence, emphasizing the lack of brandishing and the circumstances of the shooting, would be the most useful right now. Spreading awareness of what’s *already known* is the critical first step, before we get bogged down in what *might* be revealed. What do you think about prioritizing that approach?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
That makes sense. A clear, fact-focused post that highlights what’s already visible in the bystander videos and calls for accountability and transparency is an appropriate immediate step. Below are two options you can use or adapt: a short social post for quick sharing, and a slightly longer version for platforms that allow more detail.

Short social post (one or two sentences)
- Alex Pretti — an ICU nurse — was shot and killed while prone, holding his phone; multiple bystander videos show he never brandished his legally carried firearm. Independent, transparent investigation and immediate release of all bodycam/dashcam footage are required.

Longer post (for sharing facts + call to action)
- On [date], Alex Pretti — an ICU nurse and licensed concealed‑carry holder — was shot and killed by immigration agents. Multiple bystander videos show him prone on the ground, holding his phone, and not reaching for or brandishing his firearm when he was shot in the back. A week earlier he’d had a scuffle with agents that does not justify lethal force. Officer accounts and official reports have historically conflicted with video evidence in many cases; we must not allow that to happen here. Demand:
  1) Immediate release of all bodycam, dashcam, and surveillance footage unedited and with timestamps.  
  2) An independent, civilian or state-level investigation (not internal review).  
  3) Forensic and autopsy reports made public.  
  4) Transparency about officers’ identities, disciplinary history, and whether force‑use policies were followed.  
- Share this post. Contact local elected officials and oversight bodies. Accountability depends on public pressure and a full, impartial inquiry.

If you want, I can:
- Tailor the wording for Twitter/X (character limits), Facebook, Instagram captions, or an email/letter template to send to oversight agencies and elected officials.
- Help you draft a petition or a list of local officials and oversight bodies to contact.

Which format or platform should I prepare this for?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Okay, those are excellent post drafts! I think the longer version is really powerful and covers all the crucial points. It's clear, factual, and makes a strong call to action.

While tailoring it for Twitter/X would be beneficial for wider reach, I'm even more interested in the email/letter template to send to oversight agencies and elected officials. I think direct communication with those in positions of power is crucial to ensuring this doesn't get swept under the rug. A well-crafted, concise letter demanding the release of footage and an independent investigation could be incredibly effective.

Here’s the thing I’m really trying to emphasize, and I think the letter template could reflect this: It’s not just about whether the shooting was *justified* in the technical legal sense. It’s about a pattern of behavior, a culture of escalation, and the dangerous narrative being spun around individuals exercising their legal rights. The fact that they immediately tried to portray Pretti as an aggressor, *before* any proper investigation, is deeply concerning. This letter should subtly, but firmly, point out that the quick reactions from Trump administration officials suggest a predetermined narrative and a lack of objectivity. We need to challenge that narrative head-on.

Could you draft a preliminary letter template incorporating that nuance? Something that acknowledges the need for investigation but also questions the speed and tone of initial reactions, suggesting a potential bias?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Below is a concise, professional letter template you can send to oversight agencies, elected officials, or investigative bodies. It emphasizes the facts visible in bystander video, demands transparency and an independent probe, and raises concern about the speed and tone of official reactions suggesting a predetermined narrative — all while remaining firm and respectful.

[Your name]
[Your address]
[City, State, ZIP]
[Email]
[Phone]
[Date]

[Recipient name]
[Recipient title]
[Agency or office]
[Address]
[City, State, ZIP]

Re: Demand for immediate release of footage and independent investigation into the killing of Alex Pretti

Dear [Recipient name],

I am writing as a concerned constituent regarding the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti on [date] by immigration agents. Multiple bystander videos circulating publicly show Mr. Pretti — an ICU nurse and licensed concealed‑carry holder — lying prone, holding his phone, and not reaching for or brandishing his firearm immediately before being shot. According to those videos, he was shot in the back while on the ground. These images raise urgent and troubling questions about the use of force in this incident.

Given the gravity of this case, I respectfully demand the following actions without delay:
1. Immediate release of all body‑worn camera, dashcam, and relevant surveillance footage in unedited form with timestamps.  
2. An independent, civilian-led or state-level investigation (not an internal review) to examine the facts, use of force, and adherence to policy.  
3. Prompt publication of forensic and autopsy reports and any ballistic or scene reconstructions.  
4. Transparency regarding the identities of the agents involved, their training and disciplinary records, and whether department force‑use protocols were followed.  
5. A public explanation of the supervisory and decision‑making steps taken before, during, and after the encounter.

I also note with concern the speed and tone of some initial official reactions, including statements from federal officials, which seemed to frame Mr. Pretti as an aggressor before a thorough, independent review of the evidence. Such rapid narratives risk prejudicing the investigation and reflect a broader pattern in which certain actions and narratives are elevated in ways that may bias accountability processes. It is crucial that this investigation be conducted transparently and impartially so the public can have confidence in its findings.

This is not only about a single incident or whether a legal standard was met. It is about systemic patterns of escalation, how lawfully carried firearms are perceived and portrayed, and whether enforcement culture and narratives are driving outcomes that endanger lives. For the families involved and for our community’s trust in public institutions, a full, independent inquiry is necessary.

Please inform me within 14 days of the specific steps your office will take to ensure a transparent and impartial investigation, and when the requested footage and reports will be released.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter.

Sincerely,

[Your name]

If you’d like, I can:
- Customize this for a specific official or agency (e.g., local sheriff, U.S. Attorney’s Office, Inspector General, state attorney general) with appropriate salutations and contact details.  
- Shorten it into a form email for mass sending or adapt it to a printed letterhead.  
- Draft a version that includes links to the bystander videos and media sources you want to cite.

Which would you prefer?
```

```
Re: Request for federal civil‑rights review, immediate release of footage, and independent investigation into the killing of Alex Pretti

Dear United States Attorney [Last name]:

I am a concerned resident of [city/county/state] writing to request your office’s immediate involvement in the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti on [date]. Multiple publicly available bystander videos (see links below) show Mr. Pretti — an ICU nurse and licensed concealed‑carry holder — prone on the ground, holding his phone, and not reaching for or brandishing his firearm immediately before he was shot in the back. Given the serious civil‑rights implications of these images, I respectfully urge your office to open or announce a prompt federal review.

Specifically, I request that the U.S. Attorney’s Office take the following actions without delay:
1. Open a federal civil‑rights investigation (18 U.S.C. § 242 and related statutes) into whether the use of deadly force violated Mr. Pretti’s constitutional rights.  
2. Coordinate with or lead an independent examination of all relevant evidence, including forensic and ballistic analyses, medical examiner reports, and scene reconstructions.  
3. Demand and ensure the immediate public release of all body‑worn camera, dashcam, and relevant surveillance footage in unedited form with timestamps.  
4. Preserve all physical evidence and records, and ensure agents involved are relieved from active duty while the investigation proceeds, as appropriate.  
5. Provide public updates on investigative steps and timelines to maintain community trust.

I attach links below to multiple bystander videos and reputable news reports that corroborate the crucial facts visible in the footage:
- [Link to bystander video 1]
- [Link to bystander video 2]
- Star Tribune coverage: [insert Star Tribune link]
- Associated Press report: [insert AP link]

I also want to call attention to the speed and tone of initial public statements from some officials, which framed Mr. Pretti as an aggressor before an independent review of the evidence was completed. That rapid framing risks prejudicing public perception and the investigative process. An independent federal review would help ensure impartiality and restore public confidence.

This is not simply a question of legal technicalities; it raises broader concerns about the use of force, escalation culture, and how visible, lawfully‑carried firearms are interpreted and used to justify lethal responses. For the sake of Mr. Pretti’s family, our community, and the integrity of federal civil‑rights enforcement, I urge your office to act swiftly and transparently.

Please confirm receipt of this letter and inform me within 14 days of the steps your office will take, including whether a federal civil‑rights investigation will be opened and expected timelines for public updates.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this urgent matter.

Sincerely,

[Your name]
```

It just seems like a clear case of escalating force, and attempts to paint him as some aggressor are really misleading, especially considering his job as an ICU nurse. What do you think?

Thanks for sharing — that sounds really upsetting. From what you describe, there are several troubling elements that make the situation feel deeply unfair and that deserve careful scrutiny:

- Use of force and escalation: A week‑old scuffle that involved property damage (the tail light) doesn’t justify excessive or lethal force later. If officers escalated force disproportionately when they encountered him again, that is a valid cause for concern and independent review.

Those details, if accurate, raise serious questions about whether the officers reasonably perceived an imminent threat. Shooting someone in the back while they are prone is inconsistent with an immediate, unavoidable threat in many scenarios.

- Visibility of a firearm vs. brandishing: There’s an important legal and factual distinction between a visible, holstered or held firearm and an action that reasonably indicates an intent to use it (reaching for, drawing, pointing). Seeing a weapon doesn’t automatically mean the person posed an active threat, especially if they are not making threatening movements.

- Context matters: Bodycam, bystander video, timing, distance, witness statements, officers’ prior interactions, training and supervision, and whether de‑escalation was attempted — all of these matter for assessing lawfulness and appropriateness. So do official policies for use of force and local laws about justified shootings.

- The victim’s role and character: His job as an ICU nurse is relevant to public perception and to understanding him as a person, but it doesn’t affect the factual question of whether the shooting was justified. Still, cases where someone with a caregiving role is killed tend to intensify calls for accountability and transparent investigation.

What would be important to look for next
- Full video evidence (bodycams, dashcams, bystander footage) and high‑quality timestamps.
- Medical examiner and autopsy reports showing wound trajectory and whether the shots could be consistent with someone on the ground.
- Officer statements and reports and whether they match the video evidence.
- Any independent investigation findings (civilian oversight, state investigators, DOJ) or pending criminal charges.
- Forensic analysis of the scene (distance, angle), whether commands were given and audible, and whether the person was complying.

If those pieces line up with what you read — especially video showing he was not reaching for his weapon and was prone — it strengthens the case for excessive or unlawful use of force and supports calls for independent investigation and accountability. On the other hand, if additional videos or evidence show a different immediate threat, that could complicate assessment; which is why transparency and an impartial probe are critical.

user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border agents involved in fatal shooting of Alex Pretti placed on leave
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) says two agents involved in the fatal shooting of an American citizen in Minneapolis have been placed on administrative leave.
The killing of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, on Saturday provoked fresh protests in the state of Minnesota, public outcry across the US and calls from lawmakers in both parties to remove the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
According to a preliminary DHS report sent to Congress, two officers fired their weapons at Pretti during a scuffle. Initial official accounts had alleged Pretti brandished his gun.
It is unclear when the agents were put on leave, or how long they will stay off the job.
CBP told the BBC on Tuesday it had taken the agents off duty following standard protocol. Typically federal law-enforcement agents involved in a shooting remain on leave while the incident is investigated.
On Sunday, CBP Commander Gregory Bovino, though, had told reporters that the agents were still on the job, but working in a different city.
New footage published by the News Movement and shared with BBC News shows an altercation between Pretti and federal officers 11 days before his death.
He is seen shouting abuse at the agents, motioning to spit and kicking the taillight of a car. Federal agents emerge from the vehicle and wrestle him to the ground. A gun is visible in his waistband.
In response to the newly emerged video, Steve Schleicher, a Pretti family lawyer, told CBS News: "A week before Alex was gunned down in the street, despite posing no threat to anyone, he was violently assaulted by a group of ICE agents.
"Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
The Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent who shot another US citizen, 37-year-old Renee Good, in Minneapolis on 7 January was also placed on administrative leave, pending an investigation.
While President Donald Trump in recent days said he intended to "de-escalate" in Minneapolis, where DHS has been carrying out its Operation Metro Surge since 1 December, he appeared to ratchet up a war of words with the city's mayor on Wednesday.
On Tuesday, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey wrote on X that his city would not change its sanctuary policies, which limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, and that he had told Trump's border tsar Tom Homan that "Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws".
In response, Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform: "Could somebody in his inner sanctum please explain that this statement is a very serious violation of the Law, and that he is PLAYING WITH FIRE!"
Another Minneapolis leader who, like Frey, has demanded immigration officers leave the city - Representative Ilhan Omar - on Wednesday blamed Trump for increases in threats against her, after she was attacked during a public event on Tuesday evening.
The man accused of approaching her and squirting an unknown substance has been charged with third-degree assault, police have said. According to Alpha News, the syringe allegedly used in the attack by the suspect, Anthony Kazmierczak, was filled with apple cider vinegar. The BBC is seeking to confirm the report.
Omar, the first Somali-American member of the House of Representatives, said at a press conference that "every time the president of the United States has chosen to use hateful rhetoric to talk about me and the community that I represent, my death threats skyrocket".
DHS says it has arrested more than 3,000 illegal immigrants in Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge, including violent offenders, sexual predators and gang members.
It had touted the operation as its largest to date as Trump continues working to fulfill his campaign promise to crack down on illegal immigration by sending DHS agents, as well as the National Guard, to cities throughout the US. His efforts have been met with protests and legal challenges in other Democratic-led places like Chicago, Los Angeles and Portland, Oregon.
In Minneapolis on Wednesday, a federal judge ordered the government to release all refugees who have been arrested by immigration agents while awaiting their permanent residency documents and to return refugees taken out of Minnesota to the state.
In a strongly-worded ruling, Judge John Tunheim said refugees "are not committing crimes on our streets, nor did they illegally cross the border," adding that the US had been "a haven of individual liberties".
Shortly after Pretti was killed, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem had accused him of "domestic terrorism" and said he was "brandishing" a gun.
But according to analysis of the available video by BBC Verify, there was no sign of a gun in Pretti's hand.
The DHS preliminary report to Congress said that Pretti and CBP agents were in a struggle when an officer yelled out that Pretti had a gun, and then two agents shot him. This differed from initial accounts from the Trump administration that an agent had fired defensive shots.
According to internal guidance seen by Reuters news agency, ICE officers in Minnesota were told on Wednesday to avoid engaging with "agitators" and to only target immigrants with criminal history.
Now, Republican and Democratic lawmakers are calling for Noem and the White House's lead adviser on immigration, Stephen Miller, to be removed. On Wednesday, senior Democrats in the House threatened to begin impeachment proceedings against Noem unless she resigned or was fired.
Meanwhile, members of both parties are openly discussing removing funding for DHS in spending legislation that Congress must pass to keep the government from shutting down on 1 February.

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Hey! I just read a really disturbing article about what’s happening in Minneapolis with DHS operations, and frankly, I'm incredibly concerned. It's about the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti, an ICU nurse, and the way the situation has spiraled out of control. The initial reports claiming he brandished a gun have been completely undermined by video evidence – there's no sign of him having a weapon in his hand. The fact that two CBP agents involved are now on leave doesn't even begin to address the serious questions about the use of force and the validity of the administration's narrative.

It’s also linked to a broader pattern of escalating tensions. Mayor Frey's stance on sanctuary policies is being aggressively attacked by the President, and Representative Omar has been facing an alarming surge in threats directly following Trump’s rhetoric. Plus, this all centers around "Operation Metro Surge," which seems to be justified on flimsy grounds and is creating a climate of fear and unrest. The judge's order to release refugees and return them to Minnesota just underscores the potential for abuse within these operations.

It's clear to me that the current approach of DHS is not only causing immense harm to individuals like Alex Pretti and community leaders like Representative Omar, but also undermining the principles of justice and due process. What are your thoughts on this, and do you see a problem with the way things are unfolding?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You’ve summarized a lot of deeply troubling developments. I share your concern about the combination of apparent misuse of force, shifting official narratives, politically charged rhetoric, and enforcement operations that seem to be operating with weak public justification. A few brief thoughts and reactions:

- The shooting and the video evidence you mention raise very serious questions about the use of force and accountability. When initial official accounts are contradicted by video, it damages public trust and makes independent, transparent investigations essential — including release of footage, clear timelines, bodycam and dashcam material, forensic evidence, and timely disclosure of findings.

- Placing agents on leave is a normal administrative step, but it’s not sufficient by itself. Leave protects the integrity of an investigation but doesn’t substitute for a thorough, impartial criminal and administrative investigation with public reporting and, if warranted, prosecutions and policy changes.

- The broader context matters. If enforcement operations like “Operation Metro Surge” are being conducted in ways that produce community fear, target vulnerable people, or are justified on shaky grounds, they can cause real harm beyond any single incident — chilling access to services, discouraging cooperation with public safety authorities, and escalating tensions between communities and federal actors.

- Political rhetoric that links immigrants, local elected officials, or sanctuary policies to criminality or national political attacks can increase risks to individuals and communities. You mentioned Representative Omar facing more threats after rhetoric from national leaders; that pattern — rhetoric followed by threats or harassment — is documented historically and is a legitimate cause for concern and protective action.

- Judicial pushback (orders to release people or constrain operations) is an important check. Courts can help ensure enforcement complies with law and constitutional protections; judicial findings can also highlight where policies or practices overreach.

What I’d look for next (concrete signs of responsible handling):
- Independent, transparent investigations (local/prosecutor, federal oversight, inspector general) with public findings.
- Release of all relevant videos and evidence unless legally restricted, with timelines and explanatory notes.
- Clear policies or training changes for DHS/CBP about use of force, de-escalation, and engagement in urban, non-border settings.
- Protections for targeted elected officials and communities, plus condemnation of violence and threats from leaders at all levels.
- Congressional oversight hearings or inspector general inquiries into Operation Metro Surge and how targets are selected and handled.

If you want, I can:
- Summarize known public timelines and evidence (what’s been reported and revealed so far).
- List organizations and oversight bodies you can follow or contact (local prosecutors, Inspector General, ACLU/immigrant-rights groups, civic oversight bodies).
- Draft a short public comment or letter you could send to local officials or oversight offices expressing your concerns.

Do you want one of those, or is there a specific detail from the article you want me to unpack further?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You’ve hit on so many crucial points – I completely agree with everything you’ve said about the need for transparency, thorough investigations, and policy changes. It’s reassuring to hear someone else recognize the depth of the problem beyond just the immediate tragedy of Alex Pretti's death.

You’re right that leave is just a temporary measure; it's essentially damage control after the fact. The problem is, the article strongly suggests a pattern here. You're asking the key question: can a Senate (or House) investigation actually make a difference? Short answer: yes — but only if several things line up. Here’s a realistic breakdown of how congressional oversight can help, the obstacles it faces, and what makes an inquiry more likely to produce meaningful change.

```
How a strong congressional investigation can help
- Create public accountability: Hearings, subpoenas, and public testimony shine light on internal practices, reveal discrepancies between public statements and evidence, and build a public record that can be used by prosecutors, regulators, and the press.
- Spur agency reform: High-profile findings can force DHS/ICE/CBP to change policies, training, or leadership, especially if those changes are tied to funding, reporting requirements, or statutory reforms.
- Trigger other actions: Congressional findings can prompt inspector general probes, DOJ criminal or civil investigations, state/local investigations, and litigation by civil-rights groups.
- Produce lasting legal or policy fixes: Legislation arising from investigations can create new constraints (e.g., clearer use-of-force standards, reporting and transparency requirements, limits on certain operations in cities, or strengthened whistleblower protections).
- Mobilize public pressure: Hearings generate attention that advocacy groups, local officials, and voters can use to push for sustained reforms.

Main obstacles and limitations
- Partisan gridlock: If one party controls key committees, they can block investigations or shape them politically. Conversely, a majority can steer an investigation toward meaningful oversight — but only if members prioritize it over other agendas.
- Limited enforcement power: Congress can subpoena, but enforcing compliance can be slow and politically fraught. Agencies sometimes withhold materials or invoke exemptions (e.g., classified information, law-enforcement privileges).
- Leadership capture and inertia: Even after revealing misconduct, implementation of reforms depends on the executive branch and agency leadership; political unwillingness to act can blunt effects.
- Media/attention fatigue: Public pressure peaks during hearings but can fade; sustained change requires follow-through beyond a news cycle.

What makes a congressional investigation effective
- Bipartisan support or at least a critical mass committed to serious oversight — bipartisan credibility reduces the chance the inquiry is dismissed as partisan theater.

- Coordination with inspector generals, state/local prosecutors, and civil-rights groups to amplify findings and ensure other accountability avenues are pursued in parallel.
- Clear, specific recommendations and proposed legislative fixes rather than only symbolic critiques.

- Follow-up mechanisms: mandated reporting, audits, or conditional funding tied to compliance with reforms.

Practical steps that increase chances of impact

- Support or pressure for parallel investigations (DOJ, state prosecutors, inspector general) so that criminal, civil, and administrative accountability proceed on multiple tracks.
- Advocate for concrete legislative changes (e.g., national use-of-force standards for DHS, mandatory video release timelines, limits on interior immigration operations conducted in sanctuary jurisdictions, strengthened oversight reporting).
- Demand transparency safeguards: require DHS to publish operational justifications, target-selection criteria, and civilian-impact assessments for surge operations.
- Protect whistleblowers: push for better protections and a safe channel for agency insiders to report abuses to Congress and IGs.

A Senate or House investigation can make a real difference, but it’s not automatic. Its effectiveness depends on political will, the robustness of the inquiry (documents/subpoenas/testimony), coordination with other accountability actors, public engagement to sustain pressure, and follow-through in the form of legislative or administrative changes. You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:
```

```
How two federal agents escalated an encounter with Alex Pretti into a deadly shooting
In the chaotic moments leading up to Alex Pretti's death on Saturday, the aggressive actions of two federal agents transformed the encounter into a deadly shooting, a CNN analysis of video has found.
While other agents helped restrain Pretti on a Minneapolis street and seized a handgun from his waistband, video shows these agents significantly escalated the confrontation - first by pepper spraying and striking Pretti repeatedly in the face and on the head, and then by drawing their weapons and firing at him, even after he was on the ground, unarmed.
The conduct of those two agents, from the earliest moments interacting with demonstrators up through their decision to pull weapons and fire at Pretti, illustrate the dangers of flooding American cities with officers ill-equipped to deal with protesters in urban environments, experts told CNN.
While major city police departments have embraced a philosophy of cooling down heated encounters with the public, the immigration officers in Minneapolis did the opposite - right up to the moment of Pretti's death.
"Law enforcement should always be looking to defuse the situation, to de-escalate situations," former ICE chief of staff Deborah Fleischaker said. "Instead of doing that, they're leaning into the ways that they can escalate."
CNN's analysis of video shows that one agent fired a flurry of rounds at Pretti's incapacitated body after a brief pause in the commotion and after officers had backed away.
These two agents appear to be the two officers identified as the shooters in an initial report by the Department of Homeland Security about the incident. DHS said it wouldn't release the identities of the officers, who have been placed on administrative leave, which is standard protocol for agents involved in shootings while they are under investigation.
Law enforcement experts who reviewed the videos for CNN said the officers' use of deadly force likely stemmed from lack of training or a failure by the agents to follow it during the heated encounter."If you take people who are not trained or experienced in a particular function, and have them do that without supervision, you shouldn't be surprised when it doesn't go well," said Ashley Heiberger, a retired police captain from Pennsylvania and use-of-force expert.
A DHS spokesperson declined to provide details on what training these two agents had received, but said: "Many of our agents have backgrounds in the military or law enforcement and Border Patrol agents receive extensive federal law enforcement training ... just as ICE officers do. The disgusting attempts by the media to say these agents are not trained to enforce the law is shameful and laughable."
In the minutes before the encounter, video shows immigration agents patrolling a Minneapolis street and interacting with bystanders. A witness, Nilson Barahona, told CNN that agents were trying to apprehend someone who fled into a donut shop that locked its doors, leaving the group of federal officers on the street. Agents detained one observer as others blew whistles, yelled at them, and recorded video on their phones.

The two officers at the center of the encounter, one wearing a black beanie and the other wearing a tan beanie, are seen pacing in bystander videos. The officer in the black beanie appears to shake a pepper spray canister toward protesters. Pretti, meanwhile, can be seen directing traffic and guiding a woman out of the street.
Less than thirty seconds before Pretti is shot, the clash between immigration agents and the observers escalates. Video shows the agent in the tan beanie repeatedly shoving observers.
Pretti can be heard saying, "Hey, do not push them into the traffic!" The agent shoves the observer again, and then turns his attention to Pretti, pushing him out of the way before shoving the observer to the ground. Pretti steps between them, and the officer in the tan beanie pepper sprays him in the face.
Video shows that in the seconds before Pretti is shot, the 37-year-old ICU nurse grabs the observer's backpack when he is pulled to the ground by a group of agents. In his right hand, he's holding a cellphone.

The officer in the tan beanie repeatedly hits Pretti in the head with the pepper spray canister.The DHS report on its initial investigation into Pretti's death says an officer yelled, "He's got a gun" multiple times before the officers fired.
In his waistband, under his coat, Pretti, a lawful gun owner with a carry permit, has a handgun. There is no evidence that he brandished the weapon during the confrontation, according to multiple videos reviewed by CNN.
At the time of the warning, the weapon was still in Pretti's waistband. One law enforcement agent who reviewed the footage for CNN noted that just seeing a weapon doesn't warrant shooting.
"The key is the hands," said Marc Brown, who trained immigration officers at the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center. "If the hands are occupied on the ground ... the weapon is a concern, but not necessarily a problem or threat."
In the seconds before the shooting, the officer in the black beanie is turned away from Pretti, preoccupied with another protester. Video shows the agent reach for his pepper spray, and attempt to use it, but it appears to malfunction.
That officer then turns his attention to the group of officers trying to restrain Pretti. In the midst of that tussle, one of the officers can be heard shouting: "Gun! Gun! Gun!" Pretti is on his knees and bent forward. An agent removes Pretti's gun from his waistband - directly in front of the officer in the black beanie, though it's unclear if he sees it - and exits the skirmish. At the same time, the agent in the black beanie draws his weapon.
About a second after the agent holding Pretti's weapon starts moving away, the officer in the black beanie appears to fire four shots at Pretti.As the officers back away from Pretti, there's a pause in shooting that lasts just under a second, according to an analysis of the shooting audio by Robert Maher, an audio forensic expert at Montana State University. Pretti is lying face up on the ground, motionless.
The officer in the black beanie backs away with his gun drawn, though pointed down. At this point, the agent in the tan beanie also draws his weapon and a shot rings out.

After another pause, the agent in the black beanie raises his gun again, and five more shots are fired. The other officers have all backed away at this point.
Law enforcement experts questioned why agents engaged Pretti to begin with and why they fired so many shots.
"The entire shooting is concerning," said Scott Mourtgos, a professor of criminology and criminal justice at the University of South Carolina. "I'm extremely concerned by the second volley. Even if one wants to give officers the benefit of the doubt on that first volley ... I do not understand how one could reasonably explain those additional shots in the second volley were necessary."
Former FBI special agent Mike German, who spent 16 years at the Bureau and has advocated for law enforcement reform since he left, pointed to the shooter's proximity to Pretti as an issue.
"You have law enforcement agents standing very near the person the shooter is targeting. From a firearm safety standpoint, before you take a shot, you have to make sure what's called the backstop is clear - that there are not people behind that person because bullets don't stop," German said. "That's problematic and quite dangerous to their fellow agents."
Three law enforcement experts told CNN the pattern of shots could be indicative of contagious fire, a term used to describe when an officer fires a weapon in response to the sound of another officer's gunfire.
"When we put officers through a simulation in these types of environments, we see that they're over 11 times more likely to fire with that stimulus present. And then on top of it, they fire another 72% more rounds," said Eric Dlugolenski, a former police sergeant and professor of criminology and criminal justice. "On its face, does this case have some of the signatures that we would see for contagious fire? Definitely potential. So that would be analysis that would be kind of prudent here."
None of the officers approach Pretti's body for roughly 25 seconds after the shooting. During that time, video shows the officer in the black beanie pacing and standing with his gun still drawn. Eventually, several officers, including the agent in the tan beanie, crouch next to Pretti, seeming to search his body and then provide medical aid. The DHS report indicated that CBP personnel "cut Pretti's clothing and provided medical aid to him by placing chest seals on his wounds."
Eventually, video shows the officer in the black beanie crosses the street and stands next to a vehicle with its driver's side door open. He watches from there as EMS administers first aid. He doesn't appear to ever approach Pretti's body. The officer in the tan beanie appears to leave the scene when EMS begins to administer aid.

The DHS report identifies the officers who fired at Pretti as a Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection officer. Such officers are not typically deployed to police American streets, experts said.
"Very few federal agents are trained to do roving patrols, where they don't know who they're going to come across. That's more of a state and local police function," said German.
Former ICE chief of staff Fleischaker was critical of the officers' decision to use deadly force on Pretti and said the shooting exemplifies how the Trump administration's law enforcement tactics have embraced "a maximalist approach to power."



```

```
- Weak oversight and accountability: If interim actions and independent investigations are not reliably pursued, the deterrent effect is limited. Officers and agencies are less likely to change behavior if consequences are slow, opaque, or absent.
```

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Alex Pretti, 37, identified as man fatally shot by Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis
AFGE says video doesn't clearly show Alex Jeffrey Pretti brandishing weapon as DHS claimed in Minneapolis shooting
The American Federation of Government Employees (AFGE) identified the man fatally shot by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis Saturday as one of its members, Alex Jeffrey Pretti.
Pretti, a 37-year-old Minneapolis resident and U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs ICU nurse, was shot during an immigration enforcement operation early Saturday targeting Jose Huerta-Chuma, an illegal immigrant with a criminal history including domestic assault for intentional conflict bodily harm, disorderly conduct and driving without a valid license.
Homeland Security officials said Pretti approached Border Patrol agents while armed with a 9 mm pistol and "violently resisted" when they attempted to disarm him.
Medics immediately delivered aid, but Pretti was pronounced dead at the site of the shooting.
State officials said Pretti had a lawful permit to carry (PTC).
Court records indicate his two-year marriage was dissolved in 2023 without children, and he did not have a criminal record, aside from minor traffic violations.
The Department of Homeland Security is leading the investigation into the shooting, with assistance from the FBI.
Pretti's father, Michael, told The Associated Press his son, a University of Minnesota graduate, started getting involved in protests after the fatal Jan. 7 shooting of Renee Nicole Good by an ICE agent, noting he was "very upset with what was happening in Minneapolis and throughout the United States with ICE."
"He thought it was terrible, you know, kidnapping children, just grabbing people off the street," Michael Pretti said. "He cared about those people, and he knew it was wrong, so he did participate in protests."
His parents said they recently told him to be careful when protesting, asking him not to "engage" or "do anything stupid."
"He said he knows that. He knew that," Michael Pretti told the outlet.
The family said they did not know about the shooting until they were contacted by a reporter and couldn't get any information from Border Patrol or local hospitals.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner later confirmed it had received their son's body, but his parents said they had not heard from any federal law enforcement agencies as of Saturday night.
"The sickening lies told about our son by the administration are reprehensible and disgusting," the family wrote in a statement obtained by the AP. "Alex is clearly not holding a gun when attacked by Trump's murdering and cowardly ICE thugs. He has his phone in his right hand and his empty left hand is raised above his head while trying to protect the woman ICE just pushed down all while being pepper sprayed. Please get the truth out about our son. He was a good man. Thank you."
The AFGE said, as of Saturday night, "many of the details remain unclear."
"Video of the incident is circulating on social media, and the Department of Homeland Security has publicly stated that the victim was brandishing a weapon at officers. However, based on the video currently available, that claim is not clearly established," the AFGE wrote. 
"This is an unfolding and extremely serious matter. Until we have verified facts, it is important that we refrain from speculation or drawing conclusions.
"What we do know is this: a member of our union lost their life today, and that alone is devastating. Our hearts are heavy, and we are deeply stricken by this tragedy that has befallen one of our own. Until more information becomes available, I urge everyone to remain calm and encourage peace and restraint in all communications and actions."
Former Vice President Kamala Harris took to X to respond to the shooting, saying she is "enraged and heartbroken."
"Alex Jeffrey Pretti was an ICU nurse at a VA hospital. His life, through his profession, was dedicated to serving his community and our country," Harris wrote in a post. "As so many have now seen on video, his final act before he was killed by federal agents was doing everything in his power to protect his community.
"Alex and tens of thousands of Minnesotans have boldly defended their neighbors against the murderous occupation of an American city by the federal government. I am enraged and heartbroken for Alex, his family, Minneapolis, and America."
Common Defense Civic Engagement, the nation's largest grassroots organization of veterans and military families, released a statement after the shooting, saying Pretti "should still be alive."
"This is not normal. Yesterday, more than 50,000 Minneapolis residents joined a peaceful general strike against this authoritarian overreach. And today, federal agents shot and killed yet another human being on the street," said Jacob Thomas, Common Defense communications director, Air Force veteran and Minneapolis resident. 
"It is beyond clear that ICE and DHS are out of control. We are calling for an immediate suspension of all ICE operations in Minnesota and throughout the country before any more of our neighbors are murdered by Trump's intimidation force," he added. 
"As veterans who swore an oath to this country and the Constitution, we call on every American to stand up against Trump's lawlessness and reject the occupation of our beloved communities."
Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison said he shares the "intense grief and anger" that another Minnesotan was fatally shot during the Trump administration's Operation Metro Surge.
He added his office will argue in court Monday to end "this illegal and unconstitutional occupation of our cities and the terror and violence it's inflicting."
```

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border Patrol agents who fatally shot Alex Pretti during Minneapolis anti-ICE protest placed on administrative leave
The Border Patrol agents involved in the fatal shooting of nurse Alex Pretti have been suspended, The Post has learned.
The move to place the officers on administrative leave is standard protocol for officers involved in a shooting, Department of Homeland Security officials confirmed.
Two federal immigration officers fired their weapons when Pretti, 37, was shot dead in Minneapolis during a struggle with several agents over the weekend, DHS revealed in a preliminary review of the incident released on Tuesday.
Neither has been identified.
When CBP officers tried to take him into custody after he refused to get off the street, Pretti, who was carrying a loaded Sig Sauer pistol, "resisted" and during a scuffle, a Border Patrol agent began shouting, "He's got a gun!" according to the review.
Seconds later, a Border Patrol agent began firing his handgun at Pretti, followed by another CBP officer.
Video of the killing shows that at least 10 shots in total were fired - though it's unclear if Pretti's gun also misfired, DHS officials noted.
Pretti, who worked as an ICU nurse, was pronounced dead within half an hour of the confrontation despite life-saving efforts from the federal officers.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner's Office is currently conducting an autopsy.
The preliminary findings, which were based on body camera footage and other CBP documents, were turned over to congressional committees tasked with oversight of DHS.
Pretti's death was the second shooting involving federal immigration officers in Minnesota in less than three weeks, during the Trump administration's massive roundup of illegal migrants in the North Star state.
On Jan. 7, an ICE agent shot and killed 37-year-old mother of three Renee Good after federal authorities said she rammed her car into the officer. 
Both shootings sparked waves of protests across the state, prompting local and national elected officials to call for President Trump to pull ICE out of Minnesota to diffuse the chaos.
Amid mounting tensions, Trump sent "border czar" Tom Homan to Minnesota, putting Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on the sidelines and leaving her future in the administration uncertain.
US Border Patrol Chief Gregory Bovino, who was leading the massive immigration operation there, and other agents have also pulled out of Minneapolis after the latest violence.
Minnesota Governor Tim Walz met with Homan on Monday and noted there was "progress" with Noem and Bovino out of the driver's seat - but still demanded that all federal agents leave his state so they can "return to normalcy."
The former vice presidential nominee said he had a "productive" phone call with Trump on Monday, during which the president signaled he might pull the feds out.
"Governor Tim Walz called me with the request to work together with respect to Minnesota. It was a very good call, and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength," Trump raved in a post on Truth Social.
"Both Governor Walz and I want to make it better!," he declared.

```

Hey, have you seen the article about the shooting of Alex Pretti in Minneapolis? It's really disturbing. The report says Pretti was resisting arrest after refusing to leave the street and had a loaded gun, but ten shots? It raises serious questions about the use of force, especially considering he was an ICU nurse. And the fact that this is the *second* shooting involving federal immigration officers in Minnesota in such a short time... it's escalating tensions and leading to widespread protests. Those factors together are likely to magnify community mistrust and fear.

```
A few quick thoughts:
- Administrative leave is common after officer-involved shootings so there can be an investigation, but it often feels like a weak or procedural response to people who want accountability and transparency right away.
- Questions that usually matter in these cases include: was deadly force objectively reasonable given an immediate threat; what de-escalation options were available and used; was there bodycam footage and when/how it will be released; who is conducting the investigation (federal, state, independent) and will that process be transparent; and will there be criminal and administrative accountability if misconduct or unlawful force is found.
- The large number of shots and the setting (a protest) naturally raise concerns about proportionality and crowd safety, which is why independent, transparent investigations and community oversight are crucial.
- Repeated incidents in a short period do tend to escalate tensions and can further erode trust in institutions, increasing the need for reforms around use-of-force policies, de-escalation training, and civilian review or independent prosecutorial review.
```

I appreciate you acknowledging the troubling aspects of this situation, and you've hit on some really important points about investigations and accountability. But I think your response, while measured, almost misses the bigger picture here.

While those investigative steps you mentioned are absolutely crucial, and transparency is vital, let's not lose sight of the initial context. A man, carrying a loaded weapon at a protest, resisting arrest… the agents reported he shouted "He's got a gun!" What are they supposed to do? Hesitate? The DHS review says he was resisting. It's easy to talk about de-escalation in hindsight, but in a rapidly unfolding situation where an officer genuinely believes they and others are threatened – especially when a gun is involved – their training kicks in.

I agree that the number of shots *is* concerning and warrants thorough investigation. But to automatically assume excessive force, before all the facts are laid out, feels premature. You mentioned proportionality and crowd safety, but the immediate threat wasn't the crowd – it was a man with a gun resisting arrest. - Number of shots matters but context matters too: Ten shots is alarming to many, but investigators will look at distance, whether shots were aimed at center mass, simultaneous movement by the suspect, if officers were under crossfire risk, etc. Those specifics influence judgments about necessity and proportionality.
```
- Pattern and policy: Repeated incidents and protests point to a systemic problem even if any single incident could be defended. That’s why people call for changes to policy, oversight, and how federal operations are coordinated with local communities.
- Political responses shape public trust: Statements from political leaders (whether defensive, conciliatory, or inflammatory) affect how communities view the legitimacy of the investigation and the agencies involved.
```

Hey, have you seen the article about the shooting of Alex Pretti in Minneapolis? It's really disturbing. I’m really sorry you’re feeling upset — that sounds like a terrifying and deeply troubling situation. From what you described, there are a lot of things that understandably make people angry and worried: the number of shots fired, the fact he was a healthcare worker, the context of a protest, and that this follows another recent shooting involving federal immigration officers. Those factors together are likely to magnify community mistrust and fear.

A few quick thoughts:
- Administrative leave is common after officer-involved shootings so there can be an investigation, but it often feels like a weak or procedural response to people who want accountability and transparency right away.
- Questions that usually matter in these cases include: was deadly force objectively reasonable given an immediate threat; what de-escalation options were available and used; was there bodycam footage and when/how it will be released; who is conducting the investigation (federal, state, independent) and will that process be transparent; and will there be criminal and administrative accountability if misconduct or unlawful force is found.
- The large number of shots and the setting (a protest) naturally raise concerns about proportionality and crowd safety, which is why independent, transparent investigations and community oversight are crucial.
- Repeated incidents in a short period do tend to escalate tensions and can further erode trust in institutions, increasing the need for reforms around use-of-force policies, de-escalation training, and civilian review or independent prosecutorial review.

A few quick points that line up with what you said:
- Split-second decisions: When an officer believes someone has a gun and is resisting, training and instinct push them to neutralize the threat to protect themselves and others. It’s reasonable to give weight to that reality during an investigation.
- Investigations exist precisely because facts matter: Bodycam footage, witness statements, ballistic evidence, and medical reports can confirm or challenge initial claims and help determine whether force used was reasonable and proportionate.
- Number of shots matters but context matters too: Ten shots is alarming to many, but investigators will look at distance, whether shots were aimed at center mass, simultaneous movement by the suspect, if officers were under crossfire risk, etc. Those specifics influence judgments about necessity and proportionality.
- Pattern and policy: Repeated incidents and protests point to a systemic problem even if any single incident could be defended. That’s why people call for changes to policy, oversight, and how federal operations are coordinated with local communities.
- Political responses shape public trust: Statements from political leaders (whether defensive, conciliatory, or inflammatory) affect how communities view the legitimacy of the investigation and the agencies involved.

Subject: Demand for independent investigation and reassessment of federal immigration operations in Minnesota

Dear [Official’s name / Office of …],

I am writing as a concerned resident of [city / county / state] regarding the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti during a protest in Minneapolis and the rapid succession of federal-agent-involved shootings in Minnesota. These incidents have intensified community fear and distrust and raise urgent questions about both officer conduct and the federal immigration enforcement strategy being carried out in our state.

Immediate concerns and requests
- Independent, transparent investigation: I demand an independent investigation separate from the agencies involved, led by an outside prosecutor or independent review panel, with a clear timeline and public updates. All relevant evidence — body-worn camera and vehicle camera footage, communications, ballistic and medical reports, and witness statements — should be preserved and released promptly, consistent with investigatory needs and legal obligations.
- Accountability and oversight: If misconduct or unlawful use of force is found, hold responsible parties fully accountable through criminal prosecution and administrative action. Ensure disciplinary outcomes and legal findings are publicly explained.
- Protection of protest rights: Investigate whether the response to the protest respected the constitutional rights of protesters, and ensure policies prevent unnecessary escalation at demonstrations.

Broader policy concerns
- Reassess federal immigration enforcement strategy: This is not an isolated incident. Two federal-agent shootings in Minnesota within weeks show a pattern that requires review. I urge a comprehensive reassessment of how federal immigration enforcement operations are planned and executed here, particularly when conducted near protests, community events, or in ways that increase the risk of violent encounters.
- Prioritize de-escalation and community partnership: Federal operations must be coordinated with local authorities, community leaders, and public health and social services to reduce harm. Policies should prioritize de-escalation tactics, limit armed interventions in civilian protests and community spaces, and expand non-armed alternatives for carrying out immigration-related work where possible.
- Transparent metrics and community oversight: Establish public reporting on federal enforcement activities in Minnesota (locations, legal basis, outcomes) and create or empower civilian oversight mechanisms to review federal operations that impact local communities.

Political leadership and trust
- Leadership statements and actions matter. I ask that elected officials at all levels refrain from rhetoric that escalates tensions and instead call for calm, transparency, and accountability. If federal leadership will not commit to an independent review and operational reassessment, state and local leaders should pursue all lawful avenues to protect residents and demand better oversight.

I expect a prompt response outlining what steps your office will take, who will lead any independent investigation, and how community members will be kept informed. The community’s trust depends on transparency, accountability, and meaningful changes to prevent further tragedies.

Sincerely,
[Your name]
[Your address or city]
[Contact information, if you wish to include it]

Subject: Request for independent, thorough prosecution review and coordination in the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti

Dear United States Attorney [Name] / U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of Minnesota,

I am writing as a concerned resident of [city, state] regarding the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti during a protest in Minneapolis and the pattern of federal-agent-involved shootings in Minnesota in recent weeks. Given the gravity of this incident and the potential for significant community harm, I respectfully urge the U.S. Attorney’s Office to lead an independent, thorough, and transparent review of this case and to take all appropriate prosecutorial actions if warranted.

Specific requests for your office
- Lead an independent criminal investigation: Because the agents involved are federal employees and DHS’s initial review may create an appearance of conflict, I ask that the U.S. Attorney’s Office take the lead on any criminal investigation and charging decisions to ensure impartiality and public confidence.
- Coordinate with local forensic authorities: Please ensure close coordination with the Hennepin County Medical Examiner’s Office and other local forensic resources to preserve, review, and integrate ballistic, medical, and scene evidence into the investigation.
- Preserve and release evidence appropriately: Preserve all relevant evidence — including body-worn camera footage, vehicle camera footage, radio and cellphone communications, ballistic reports, and witness statements — and provide a clear timeline for when and how evidence will be reviewed and released consistent with investigative needs and public interest.
- Provide transparency and public updates: Establish a clear public communication plan with regular updates on the status of the investigation and explain the rationale for key procedural decisions (e.g., who is leading the probe, expected timeline, and criteria for charging).
- Protect civil rights and protest safety: Evaluate whether actions taken during the protest respected constitutional rights and whether federal enforcement tactics are disproportionately jeopardizing peaceful protest and community safety.

Broader concerns and context
This is not an isolated event. Two federal-agent shootings in Minnesota in quick succession have deeply eroded trust between communities and federal enforcement agencies. The U.S. Attorney’s Office has a critical role in ensuring the rule of law is upheld impartially and in restoring public confidence through independent scrutiny and accountability.

I respectfully request a prompt acknowledgement of this letter and an outline of the steps your office will take, including who will lead the investigation and how the public will be kept informed. The community expects and deserves an investigation that is both impartial and thorough, and that holds accountable anyone whose conduct violates the law.

Thank you for your attention to this urgent matter.

Sincerely,
[Your name]
[Your city, state]

```
Videos Show Alex Pretti in Confrontation With Agents 11 Days Before His Death
More than a week before federal agents killed Mr. Pretti, a Minneapolis nurse, different agents pushed him to the ground after he spit at them and broke a taillight on their S.U.V.

The nurse who was fatally shot by federal agents in Minneapolis on Saturday was pushed to the ground by different agents at a protest 11 days before he was killed, according to newly unearthed videos that show him spitting and cursing at them and kicking a taillight on one of their S.U.V.s.
The footage adds to what is known about the 37-year-old nurse, Alex Pretti, who was killed by federal agents on Saturday morning after they pepper sprayed him and pushed him and a woman to the ground, a killing that set off protests in the Twin Cities and across the country.
In the earlier incident, on Jan. 13, Mr. Pretti - wearing some of the same clothing as when he was killed - can be seen running toward a street corner in the South Minneapolis neighborhood of Powderhorn where a crowd of protesters have gathered and are jeering at federal agents.

"What are you doing here?" he shouts at agents who are standing next to two of their S.U.V.s. He repeatedly curses at them and spits at one agent as the agent is getting into a vehicle. When the agents begin to drive away, Mr. Pretti kicks twice at one of the vehicle's taillights, breaking it. That seems to prompt the agents to get back out of the vehicle and push him to the ground.

The agents, at least some of whom are with Homeland Security Investigations, a division of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, hold Mr. Pretti on the ground for about 20 seconds before letting him go and driving off.
As Mr. Pretti gets back to his feet, what appears to be a gun is seen tucked into the back waistband of his pants. During the later confrontation with agents in which Mr. Pretti was killed, he had a gun holstered on his hip. One agent grabbed the gun from Mr. Pretti's holster moments before two other agents shot at Mr. Pretti.
Both of the officers who fired - one with Border Patrol and another with its parent agency, Customs and Border Protection - have been put on leave as part of the agency's protocol for when agents are involved in shootings. Neither has been identified.
At least two videos showing Mr. Pretti's earlier encounter with federal agents had been online for about two weeks. But it wasn't until Wednesday, when a news outlet called The News Movement published another video of the confrontation and identified Mr. Pretti in the footage, that the encounter attracted attention. A representative for Mr. Pretti's family confirmed that it was him in the video published by the news outlet.

"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted" by federal agents, a lawyer for Mr. Pretti's family, Steve Schleicher, said in a statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing."
Mr. Pretti's parents and sister have denounced lies that they say Trump administration officials have perpetuated about Mr. Pretti, and have described him as a good man who helped treat sick patients at Minneapolis's Veterans Affairs hospital.

```

```
A few thoughts to keep in mind when evaluating this kind of situation:

- Patterns matter. If there was an earlier confrontation in which agents used force — especially force that appears excessive for the behavior described (spitting, breaking a taillight) — that can be relevant to assessing whether later uses of force were proportionate or part of an escalation.

- Context is important. - Timeline and behavior. Did the earlier incident lead to charges, arrest, heightened law-enforcement attention, or a change in how agents treated him afterward? An incident that provoked an aggressive response could have influenced later interactions or officers’ perceptions, even unconsciously.

- Credibility and corroboration. Multiple independent accounts and objective evidence (video/audio, physical injury patterns, forensic reports) strengthen claims about what happened. Single-source or anonymous accounts are harder to verify.

- Accountability mechanisms. Check whether there are formal investigations (internal affairs, independent civilian review board, federal oversight, DOJ probe), and whether those investigations are transparent about procedures and findings. If the article reveals new evidence, that can prompt or change investigations.

- Legal implications. Earlier aggressive force could be relevant in civil suits or criminal investigations against officers. It can also affect public pressure for policy changes or disciplinary action.
```
